questions.text: I naturally claim the most visible and consequential role in any effort.
I read social subtext as naturally as I read printed text.
I find it difficult to sit back when I have a clear idea of what should be done.
I find that I have to consciously remind myself to pay attention to social cues.
I feel uncomfortable when I leave things half-finished at the end of the day.
I account for travel time, transitions, and setup when planning my schedule.
I would invest my inheritance in a cause rather than spend it on myself.
I detect when someone brings up a tangential topic to distract from something that was about to be discussed.
I feel that a flexible, exploratory approach to problem-solving leads to more creative outcomes.
I find that my ability to separate emotional content from factual content during human crises is what makes my help durable and reliable.
I prefer that the team shares relationships with external partners.
I hold the view that every person's first responsibility is to themselves.
I hold the view that emotional distance during a crisis is what allows me to think clearly enough to actually be useful.
I rarely experience the physiological markers of stress even in stressful situations.
I believe that the depth of my emotional experience gives life its meaning, even when it's painful.
I do not naturally sense when a group has collectively moved on from a topic that one person still wants to discuss.
I experience frustration as a mild irritation that fades quickly.
I trust that the things that truly matter will stay in my mind without a list.
I tend to meet good news with quiet pleasure rather than visible elation.
I do not naturally register the social weight of who introduces whom in a new setting.
I notice that I instinctively assign myself the most critical tasks.
I keep my options open because my future self may see things differently.
I can read the atmosphere of a meeting within seconds of walking in.
I hold the view that the moral concern I feel for those in pain is the most authentic expression of who I am at my core.
I make sure my workspace is set up before I begin each work session.
I am cautious about overruling others, even when the stakes are high.
I hold the view that my dietary choices should reflect my own health goals, not others' preferences.
I would turn down a dream job if accepting it meant abandoning someone who depends on me.
I would decline an award or honor and redirect attention to the team.
I notice that strong emotions can make me feel physically unsteady.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most forcefully reminds me of what I owe to others.
I believe that my willingness to defer is valuable to those around me.
I experience the first bite of food when I'm truly hungry as an almost overwhelming sensory event.
I routinely upgrade my skills specifically to increase my market value.
I hold the view that a structured, rational framework for engaging with human pain is not a substitute for compassion but a complement that makes compassion more productive.
I feel that the degree to which I prioritize addressing suffering is the most transparent window into my real values.
